1:1.text: City. David had conquered many. Jerusalem was long considered as the finest city in those parts.

Tributary. It had been so to the Assyrians, Egyptians, and Chaldeans, 4 Kings 24:1. From this and similar passages, it would seem that the city was still existing: yet in others it appears to have been demolished. Here then the prophet declares what it had been: (Calmet) unless he wrote part after the death of Josias.
The beholders are astonished at the change and misery of the city.

1:2.text: Friends, who had made a league with Sedecias, Lamentations 27:3., and 48:26.

1:3.text: Rest. Many returning to join Godolias, Lamentations 40:7.
The Jews who beheld their brethren led away to Babylon, retired into Egypt, but were in misery.

1:4.text: Feast, thrice a-year. This was the most charming sight, when all the nation met to adore God, and to renew their friendship with one another.

1:5.text: Lords. Literally, "at the head," (Haydock) which Moses had threatened, Deuteronomy 26:1, 43.

1:6.text: Beauty; princes' palaces, but particularly the temple, ver. 10.
Rams, fleeing from place to place to seek relief.

1:7.text: Of all. She compares her past happiness with her present chastisement.

1:7.src: Juvenal v.; Seneca, apud St. Augustine, City of God 6:11.
1:7.text: Sabbaths, or days of rest. The pagans derided them as so much lost time. Ignava et partem vitae non attigit ullam.
If none of their legislators thought of such an institution, it was because they had not the spirit of Moses: their feasts were dissolute.

1:8.text: Unstable. Hebrew also, "removed," (Haydock) like a woman unclean.

Such were excluded from places of prayer, and were not allowed to touch a sacred book, or to pronounce God's name. Their husbands could not look at their face, nor give them any thing, but laid it down for them to take.
No condition could be more distressing.

1:9.text: End in her prosperity, to avert this misfortune.
Idolatry is a spiritual adultery, (Worthington) and one of the worst species of filth.

1:10.text: Church. Deuteronomy 33:1., and Ezechiel 44:9. The Chaldeans disregarded the ordinance.

1:11.text: O. Hebrew of the Masorets, "It is."
Protestants, "Is it nothing to you, all?" etc.
But the Vulgate is much clearer, and approved by many Protestants, lu being often used as an exclamation, Genesis 17:18.
Vintage. He has plundered all, ver. 22.
The king took a great deal, and his general the rest, 4 Kings xxiv., and xxv.

1:12.text: O. Hebrew of the Masorets, "It is."
Protestants, "Is it nothing to you, all?" etc.
But the Vulgate is much clearer, and approved by many Protestants, lu being often used as an exclamation, Genesis 17:18.
Vintage. He has plundered all, ver. 22.
The king took a great deal, and his general the rest, 4 Kings xxiv., and xxv.

1:13.text: I am like one in a burning fever, Ezechiel 24:4.
Chastised. Literally, "instructed." This is the good effect of affliction.

1:14.text: Watched. This metaphor is not too harsh, Lamentations 31:28. The Masorets prefer, (Calmet) "is bound by his hand." (Protestants) But miskad is explained (Haydock) by the Septuagint, etc., in the sense of the Vulgate. God lays the yoke on my neck suddenly. My iniquities are like bands, and Nabuchodonosor has power over me.

1:15.text: Time of vengeance. All is animated. Hebrew also, "a troop" of Chaldeans, Lamentations 2:22.
Juda. God, as the first cause, punishes the Jews by war.

1:17.text: Then. They surround the city, to starve the inhabitants, ver. 8.

1:19.text: Me. Egypt attempted to relieve Juda, to no purpose, ver. 2.
It could not, or at least did not, prove of any service to the Jews, Lamentations 2:18.

1:20.text: Ubique pavor et plurima mortis imago.

1:21.text: Done it. They conclude that I am cast off for ever. But when I shall be comforted, their turn will come; (Calmet) or rather they will feel the scourge soon after me.

1:21.src: Haydock; Chap. 48:26., etc., and Ezechiel xxv., etc.

1:22.text: Let. He prays not for their ruin, but predicts it; and wishes rather that they would be converted.

2:1.text: Obscurity. He continues to bewail the misery of Jerusalem.
Stool; the temple, and the land. The ark fell not into the hands of the enemy.
The punishment which the Lord permits, is justly ascribed to Him.

2:2.text: Unclean, or treated it as such.
Septuagint, "he hath accounted profane the kings."
Joakim, Jechonias, Sedecias, and the royal family, were exposed to the greatest ignominy and sufferings.

2:3.text: Horn: beauty and power, (Haydock) the two kingdoms, the fortresses, (Calmet) and all their strength, denoted by horns.
Hand; refusing us protection, and aiding the Chaldeans.

2:5.text: Women, suffering them to be abused, Lamentations 5:11, 13.

2:6.text: Tent: the temple, with the same indifference as if it had been a hut, built to guard the fruit of a garden, Isaias 5:5., and Psalm 79:13.
Sabbaths. The Jews rested, but could offer no sacrifices in captivity.
Priest. Saraias was slain, and Sedecias imprisoned, etc., Lamentations 52:10.

2:7.text: Cursed, or suffered it to be polluted, (Worthington) as he looked on it with horror, after it had been profaned by Achaz, etc.
Feast. What a contrast! The temple used to resound with songs of praise and music: the Chaldeans fill it with insolent shouts of victory.

2:8.text: Line, to level it with the ground, (Isaias 34:11.; Calmet) or to treat it with just severity.

Bulwark. Literally, "the first wall," (Haydock) or ditch, lined with palisades. Alexander [the Great] ordered the towers to be levelled, and the horses' manes to be cut, when Hephaestion died, to denote the general sorrow.

2:9.text: Among, as slaves, or in prison.
Law has been neglected; and now it cannot be observed, as to the ceremonial part. There are no public instructions.
No vision. When Jeremias was consulted, he had to pray for ten days, Lamentations 42:7.

2:11.text: Earth, by an overflowing of the bile, occasioned by grief, Job 16:14.

2:13.text: Sea. This is an hyperbole, to express the greatness of sorrow, as the sea surpasses all other waters.

2:14.text: Revelations. Hebrew Masoth, "burdens" for the enemy. This sentence ought to come before and they, etc., as it is in the Vulgate.

2:16.text: Mouth, with scorn, Isaias 57:4., and Psalm 34:21.

2:17.text: Old, by Moses, (Deuteronomy 28:15, 49., and Leviticus 26:14.) Micheas, (chap. 26:18.) Holda, etc.

2:18.text: Upon. Hebrew and Septuagint, "O wall," etc., ver. 8.

2:19.text: Watches. Jerusalem is here represented in the midst of danger and misery.

2:20.text: Long; quite small, Psalm 38:9. This has been denounced, Lamentations 19:9., and Deuteronomy 28:53. (Calmet) It took place at Samaria, and in the last siege of Jerusalem, (Josephus, Jewish Wars vii., and viii.; Worthington) as well as at this time.

2:22.text: About. The troops of the enemy resemble those multitudes, which come from all parts to Jerusalem. Many kings could not raise such an army.

3:1.text: Man. Jeremias had a share in the common misery, (Worthington) and bewails his own condition, as a figure of Christ, Psalm 87:16., and Isaias 53:3.
His disciples must expect to suffer.

3:2.text: Led, or driven me with the rod.
God employs two, Zacharias 11:7. That of rigour was reserved for this prophet; (chap. 38.) none of them suffered more.

3:4.text: Old and wrinkled, the effect of misery.

3:5.text: Gall. Septuagint, "head." Chaldean, "he hath seized the chief," Job 16:13. He speaks in the name of the besieged, who had been threatened with this punishment, ver. 19., and Lamentations 8:14.
And labour. Nabuzardan ransacked the city worse than his master, (Worthington) if the latter was at all present.

3:6.text: Ever indeed, Ecclesiastes 12:5., and Psalm 48:12. Jeremias (Jeremias 38:6.) was in imminent danger.

3:8.text: Prayer. God would not allow him to pray for the people, Lamentations 7:16.

3:9.text: Stones. There is no passage, Job 19:8.

3:10.text: Places. This may refer to the prophet or to the people.

3:12.text: Arrows. Such places were common where shooting was practised, 1 Kings 20:36., and Job 16:11.

3:14.text: Song. True prophets were derided on account of impostors, and because their declarations were unpleasant, etc., Lamentations 17:15., and Ezechiel 12:22.

3:15.text: Wormwood, or a bitter poisonous herb, Lamentations 9:26., and Deuteronomy 29:18.

3:16.text: One. Hebrew, "against a stone." My bread is full of them, Psalm 101:10.
He describes his afflictions, as if his teeth had been broken.

3:21.text: Hope. The remembrance fills him with grief and hope, Lamentations 20:12.

3:23.text: New. Novi should be novae, to agree with miserationes.

3:27.text: Yoke. Afflictions endured for justice sake ensure a blessing.
All may derive great benefit from suffering.

3:29.text: Hope. He does not doubt, but confides with great humility.

3:30.text: Him. We cannot verify this of the prophet as we can of Christ, (Matthew 26:62.; Calmet) to whom this particularly refers.

3:33.text: Men. He punishes with regret, Ezechiel 18:23. Our crimes force him to chastise, ver. 36.

3:37.text: Commanded. Hebrew, "said: It cometh to pass," as if by chance. There have always been Epicureans, Ezechiel 8:12., and Psalm 93:7.
Those who deny Providence speak, ver. 30.

3:39.text: Sins? If all happened by inevitable necessity, or according to the laws of Providence, why should any one complain?

3:41.text: Up. This posture "is the testimony of a soul naturally Christian."
Our heart must accompany our hands, 1 Timothy 2:8.

3:42.text: Inexorable. Hebrew and Septuagint, "Thou hast not shewn pity."

3:43.text: Covered thyself, as if not to see our distress.

3:44.text: Cloud. Isaias 59:2., and Ecclesiasticus 35:21. These expressions are admirable.

3:47.text: Prophecy. Many would read, vastatio. Hebrew, "ruin." Septuagint, "taking away."
Protestants, "Fear and a snare is come upon us, desolation and destruction." The prophets were continually in danger.
The preaching of false prophets has brought these evils upon the people.

3:51.text: I have felt more for my people than they have themselves. Moral writers often produce this text, to shew the dangers of an unguarded glance (Calmet) at women.

3:53.text: Over me, as if I were buried.
Jeremias was in prison (chap. 38:6.) when he prayed, ver. 54., and Lamentations 20:7.

3:59.text: Judge what they have judged unjustly.

3:62.text: Lips; or language (Genesis 11:1.) thou knowest, ver. 60.

3:65.text: Buckler, to cover all the body. They shall be surrounded with misery, (Psalm 108:29.) while God will protect his servants, Psalm 5:13.
Let sorrow pierce their heart, as thy enemies are treated.

4:1.text: Colour. Hebrew, "gold." Septuagint, "silver;" (Haydock) denoting the patriarchs, (Theodoret) chiefs, (Vatable) or ornaments of the temple.
It had formerly glittered with gold; now there was nothing but smoke and ruins.

4:2.text: Best. Hebrew, "gold of Phaz," in Colchis, Genesis 2:11.
In Solomon's reign, they powdered their hair with gold dust.

4:3.text: The lamia has a face like a woman, and a body like beasts; and is cruel, yet feeds its young.
The fabulous lamia is supposed to destroy all children, (Diod. Sic. xx.; Ovid, Fast. vi.) and cannot be meant. But the sea dog, though most cruel will not neglect its young ones.

Ostrich. Hebrew henim, (Haydock) "swans," Isaias 13:21., and Job 29:14. The ostrich is said to break some of its eggs. (Elian 4:37.) -- Swans do the like, and devour their young; for which reason they are hated by the Indians.
Septuagint, "their young (drajohs) have suckled the daughters of my people, so that they can find no remedy, like," etc.
Though the ostrich has wings it never flies, but dwells in desert places. Such is the condition of the Israelites.

4:5.text: Hebrew means purple. Those who have been educated in the most delicate manner, are forced to feed on the most disgusting things, 4 Kings 6:25., and 18:27., and Deuteronomy 28:54.

4:6.text: In her. Sodom was destroyed by God. Her temporal misery was short.
Jerusalem was exposed to greater afflictions (Calmet) here, (Haydock) and her ingratitude and abominations were greater, Ezechiel 16:46.

4:7.text: Old ivory. Whiteness shews its age.

4:7.src: Virgil, Aeneid xii.; Homer, Iliad iv.; Ovid ii. Am. v.; Calmet
4:7.text: As it grows old it turns yellow, and loses much of its value. But the ancients had the art of dyeing it scarlet.
Hebrew poninim may signify (Haydock) "pearls," the shells of which are stained with a delicate red; though the epithet ruddy may mean "shining," in which sense purpureus is used.

The complexion of the Nazarites was fair, with a mixture of red, Canticle of Canticles 5:10. They were probably clothed in white, and were highly respected, 1 Machabees 3:49., Amos 2:11., and Numbers 6:18.

4:8.text: The people were naturally brown. Fasting and distress cause them to turn black, Lamentations 5:10., and Joel 2:6.
They were so changed, that old acquaintances knew not one another.

4:9.text: For. Literally, "by the barrenness of the earth."
Such a death was, no doubt, more painful than to perish quickly by the sword.

4:10.text: But hunger made them the reverse. Some think they slew their children, to prevent them being exposed to more cruel torments, (Calmet) as the people of Colchis do their sick.
Sodden: boiled or roasted; coxerunt, ver. 5., and Deuteronomy 28:55. At the last siege of Jerusalem, this barbarity was manifested. (Calmet) (Josephus, Jewish Wars 7:8.; Gr. 21.) See chap 2:20.

4:12.text: Believed. God had so often protected this city against Sennacherib, Holofernes, etc.
It had been also so strongly fortified, 2 Kings 5:6.

4:13.text: Priests. They too generally favoured (Haydock) the false prophets, Lamentations 2:26., etc. They were judges, and condemned the innocent: or exposed (Calmet) the citizens to destruction, by not warning them to amend, (Haydock) and to submit to the Chaldeans.
Impostors are called prophets, as they have the same outward appearance.

4:14.text: They. Septuagint, "Her guards have tottered in," etc.
When. Protestants, "so that men could not touch their garments," as they were defiled.
These hypocrites were afraid of touching blood, as they observed external ceremonies, while they disregarded the spirit of religion.

4:15.text: Depart. They were not ashamed to speak thus to others, or the citizens address the priests contaminated with blood. Even the Chaldeans looked upon the Jews with abhorrence, as an abandoned people.
For they. Hebrew, "but they understood not, and wandered about. They, (Calmet) the Gentiles, said."

4:16.text: They, the Jews; or rather the prophet thus describes the Chaldeans, Lamentations 5:12., and Deuteronomy 28:50.

4:17.text: Save. The Egyptians attempted it in vain, Lamentations 34:8., and 37:10.

4:18.text: Streets. There were enemies within as well as without.

4:20.src: Challoner; Isaias 53:5.; St. Augustine, City of God 18:33.
4:20.text: Christ, etc. According to the letter, is spoken of their king, who is called the Christ; that is, the anointed of the Lord. But it also relates, in the spiritual sense, to Christ our Lord, suffering for our sins.
It literally speaks of Josias, or of Sedecias.
Josias was slain by the Egyptians.

But Sedecias seems chiefly to be meant. The people were much attached to him, though he was wicked; and they expected that he would have rescued them from the power of the Chaldeans, as his league with the neighbouring Gentiles (Calmet) seemed to insure, (Haydock) if they had proved faithful.
But all was useless against the Lord.

4:21.text: Rejoice. Edom had manifested her joy at the misfortunes of Juda. The prophet hints at this with a cutting irony, Psalm 136:7., and Abdias 11.
Come, as at a feast. Edom was visited five years after the Jews, Lamentations 49:7.
Naked. Septuagint, "and shalt pour it out," (Haydock) or vomit.

4:22.text: Accomplished, and sufficiently punished by exile, Lamentations 50:20., and Isaias 40:2.
In vain wouldst thou hide them.

5:1.text: The prayer, etc. This title is not in Hebrew, Septuagint, etc. Theodoret has passed over the chapter, as if he doubted of its authenticity. It does not follow the order of Hebrew letters like the preceding, and seems to be a form of prayer for those who retired into Egypt.
Jeremias foresees what would happen, and prays as the people would do.

5:2.text: Aliens. The Idumeans seized and kept possession of the southern parts.

5:3.text: Father. Many had none surviving, and all had lost their king.

5:4.text: Water. Even this was not given for nothing.

5:6.text: Hand; engaged to serve Egyptians, Babylonians, (Calmet) or other nations, to procure sustenance.

5:7.text: Iniquities. This was the usual complaint of the Jews, (chap. 31:29.) as if they had committed no offence themselves. If any virtuous people were involved in the common ruin, they bore it with resignation, and acknowledged that they had deserved it, 1 Esdras 9:6., and 2 Esdras 1:6., and Esther 14:6., and Daniel 3:29.

5:8.text: Servants. One had command over another, Matthew 24:45. The Chaldeans were like slaves, and the race of Cham was condemned to servitude, Genesis 9:26.
The Jews had formerly dominion over Edom, etc., who now treated them so cruelly.

5:9.text: Sword. Any one might kill us.

5:12.text: Hand. Thus Leonidas was treated, after his head was cut off, by Xerxes.

5:13.text: Indecently, like the Sodomites. Hebrew, "they made the young men grind" at the mill, in their prison, (Haydock) as Samson (Judges 16:21.) and Sedecias (according to the Septuagint, Lamentations 52:11.) were forced to do. To grind is often used in a bad sense; but it is not necessary to adopt it here.
The Chaldeans treated their captives without pity or shame.
Wood; burdens, or stumbling-blocks, unless they were crucified; or, "roasted," if we admit a small alteration in the Hebrew, Lamentations 4:10.
They were forced to grind naked, and were beaten with staves.

5:14.text: Gates, where sentence was usually passed.
The Jews had judges at Babylon, (Daniel 13:5.) but not at first, nor everywhere.

5:16.text: Crown, used at feasts; (Calmet) or, we have lost the sovereign power.

5:17.text: Dim, the natural consequence of extreme want, 1 Kings 14:27.

5:18.text: Foxes, which were very common, Judges 15:4. Thus, Horace says: Agros atque lares patrios, habitandaque fana Apris relinquet et edacibus lupis.

5:21.text: Convert. Thy grace must work upon our hearts, (Calmet) before we can expect redress, (Haydock) and an end of our banishment.
Beginning, when our fathers observed the law. (St. Thomas Aquinas) (Menochius) See Lamentations 31:18.; St. Augustine, City of God ii., and 4:

5:22.text: Thou hast. We might read with an interrogation, (Haydock) in Hebrew, "Hast thou?" etc. The Jews superstitiously repeat the last verse, for fear of ending the book in an ominous manner, as they do at the end of Isaias and Malachias.
Having treated us so severely, stop thy hand.
But I perceive it will be in vain to beg for redress till the seventy years be expired.
